role_summary: Osteopathic physician; founder and first president of the Albany Movement (1961-1962) in Albany, Georgia; later the first African American president of the American Osteopathic Association.
ai_reading: The embedding's strongest read on Anderson is medical, not political: his two closest neighbors by cumulative cosine proximity in this corpus are H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, count 226) and Alfred Moldovan (entry 133, count 119), the community-health-center physician and the doctor who organized medical support for Southern campaigns. The cultural record files Anderson under "Albany Movement president"; the vector space files him first with the doctors. Beyond that medical pairing his neighbors fan out across factions that rarely cluster together, Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132, SCLC), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, Mississippi SNCC), and Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP legal), which tracks the specifically coalitional way he describes founding the movement (pulling the Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League, and SNCC field organizers into one body). A second tension shows up on the concept axes: Anderson sits almost exactly at the midpoint of the Nonviolence as Theology to Armed Self-Defense axis (position +0.006), not at the nonviolence pole his public framing would predict. His own testimony explains the split, he calls himself "a nonviolent person" yet describes keeping a pistol on the car seat driving through Newton in Baker County, where he knew of people who "have been lynched."
biographical_paragraph: William Gilchrist Anderson was born December 12, 1927 in Americus, Georgia, the same Southwest Georgia farming country he later returned to as a physician [src: 2]. He completed undergraduate study at Alabama State College in Montgomery, where he became close to Ralph David Abernathy, and entered Des Moines Still College of Osteopathy in the fall of 1952, finishing his degree in 1956 [src: 2]. He settled into practice in Albany, Georgia, and in November 1961 the city's Black organizations, SNCC field workers, the NAACP, the Ministerial Alliance, and local clubs, joined into the Albany Movement and elected Anderson its first president [src: 2][src: 3]. Through a marriage that had earlier put him in the same Atlanta circle as Martin Luther King Jr., Anderson invited King and the SCLC to Albany; King arrived in mid-December 1961, and on December 16 Anderson was jailed alongside King and Abernathy on charges of parading without a permit and obstructing the sidewalk [src: 3]. The Albany campaign is often read as a defeat because it won no signed agreement, but Anderson frames its result as a shift in attitudes rather than ordinances. He appears in the corpus alongside other Albany and Southwest Georgia figures, including Charles Melvin Sherrod (entry 121) and Shirley Miller Sherrod (entry 117), whom he later invited to the lecture series he founded. After the movement years he became, in 1994, the first African American elected national president of the American Osteopathic Association, having served eighteen years on its board, and held faculty posts as associate clinical professor at the Michigan State University College of Osteopathic Medicine and associate dean at the Kirksville College of Osteopathic Medicine in Missouri [src: 2][src: 4]. The annual civil rights lecture series he established at Michigan State, the "Slavery to Freedom" series begun in 2001, carries his name [src: 4]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 126 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]. William G. Anderson's Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript.

interview_snippets.speaker: William G. Anderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Watching SNCC organizers get arrested from his office window, Anderson describes the moment he decided to stop watching and join:
interview_snippets.quote: And I said to myself, they're doing that for me. They are trying to get people registered the boat for me, my family. So that's when I got out there and joined them.

interview_snippets.speaker: William G. Anderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: On being chosen to head the new coalition at the organizing meeting in dentist Ed Hamilton's home:
interview_snippets.quote: I was elected president, not because I had inexperienced whatsoever. I had none in several rights.

interview_snippets.speaker: William G. Anderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: During the march of more than a thousand people through Harlem, a National Guardsman raised his gun and ordered the line to stop:
interview_snippets.quote: I said to him, if you hit anybody in this line, hit me first, hit me first.

interview_snippets.speaker: William G. Anderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Jailed in the same cell as King and Abernathy, the three decided one of them had to make King's scheduled Meet the Press appearance:
interview_snippets.quote: We drew straws. We've seen the jail sale and drew straws. I drew the short straw. And Martin says, you're going to meet the press.

interview_snippets.speaker: William G. Anderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the verbal city agreement the movement won and then watched the city renege on, closing public facilities rather than integrating them:
interview_snippets.quote: It's like I won a 757 airplane and don't know how to fly a kite.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mary Jones
interview_snippets.lead_in: Mary Jones, recalling how the Albany organizations chose their leader, names Anderson directly:
interview_snippets.quote: So they chose Dr. William G. Anderson as the president of the Albany Movement.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mary Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: Mary Jenkins, explaining how she came to volunteer, places the movement's working office in Anderson's home:
interview_snippets.quote: we need some help in the office that was set up in Dr. William G. Anderson's house

gallery.alt: Brick facade of the Old Mount Zion Baptist Church in Albany, Georgia.
gallery.caption: Old Mount Zion Baptist Church in Albany, Dougherty County, Georgia, a site of Albany Movement mass meetings during the 1961 to 1962 campaign that William G. Anderson led as the movement's first president.
